title: Whakapapa of the Waterway: Exploring Ngāti Kahungunu Narratives Through English | Te Whakapapa o te Awa: Te Tūhura i ngā Kōrero o Ngāti Kahungunu mā te Ingarihi
subject: English
culturalContext: This lesson is deeply rooted in the awa (river) that flows through our rohe - Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Students will explore traditional pūrākau (stories) of Ngāti Kahungunu that speak to the creation and significance of this waterway, particularly the story of Hinepūkohurangi and her connection to the mist that rises from its waters. They will examine how these narratives contain environmental knowledge about seasonal changes, water quality indicators, and sustainable practices specific to our local ecosystem.
learningObjectives: Analyze and compare traditional Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau about Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with contemporary scientific descriptions of the same environment
Create a written piece that effectively integrates Māori cosmological perspectives with English environmental terminology
Demonstrate active listening and respectful questioning techniques through engagement with local kaumatua knowledge holders
Use descriptive language and metaphorical devices inspired by Māori oral traditions to describe local environmental features

activities.title: Pūrākau Immersion: Listening to the Awa's Story
activities.description: Students will gather at the banks of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with a local kaumatua who will share traditional stories of the waterway. They will practice active listening techniques and record key metaphors, symbols, and environmental knowledge embedded in the narratives.

activities.title: Comparative Text Analysis: Traditional Wisdom Meets Scientific Description
activities.description: Using provided texts including the book 'Ngā Pūrākau o Te Matau a Māui' and Hawke's Bay Regional Council's environmental reports on the Mangakōtukutuku catchment, students will create Venn diagrams comparing how Māori narratives and scientific documents describe the same environmental features and processes.

activities.title: Whakapapa of Place: Creating Multilingual Descriptions
activities.description: Students will select a specific feature along the awa (a pool, rapid, or bank) and create a detailed written description that incorporates both English scientific terminology and Māori metaphorical language, capturing both measurable characteristics and cultural significance.

activities.title: Digital Storytelling: Voices of the Awa
activities.description: Using iPads and recording equipment, students will create short digital narratives that blend oral history excerpts from community interviews with their own written commentary, exploring how the relationship between people and the awa has evolved over time.

resources: Local kaumatua from Ngāti Kahungunu with knowledge of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku pūrākau
The book 'Ngā Pūrākau o Te Matau a Māui' by Ngāti Kahungunu author Joseph Potangaroa
Hawke's Bay Regional Council's 'Mangakōtukutuku Catchment Environmental Report 2024'
School iPad sets with Voice Record Pro app and tripods for recording interviews

tasks: Written comparative analysis of traditional pūrākau and scientific report on one aspect of the awa
Multilingual descriptive piece of a local feature using both English and Te Reo Māori terminology
Presentation of digital narrative showing understanding of narrative techniques in both cultural traditions

nzcAlignment: English: Listening, Reading, and Viewing - Processes and strategies: Integrate sources of information, processes, and strategies to identify form and express ideas
English: Speaking, Writing, and Presenting - Ideas: Select, develop, and communicate ideas on a range of topics
Social Sciences: Understand how cultural practices reflect and express people's customs, traditions, and values
Science: Living World - Ecology: Explain how living things are suited to their particular habitat and how they respond to environmental changes
